text.title: The five phases
text.summary: Brainstorm runs five phases in fixed order: Phase 0 CONSENT GATE, Phase 1 DIVERGE (10 research agents), Phase 2 CONVERGE (synthesis), Phase 3 REVIEW (8 adversarial reviewers), Phase 4 FINALIZE. Phase 0 is the only conditional phase; it is skipped only when the user explicitly asked for a brainstorm or already approved one in this session.

section.title: Phase 0: CONSENT GATE
section.p: The consent gate is the most load-bearing phase: brainstorm is expensive (18+ subagents, hundreds of recommendations) and the orchestrator's auto-routing is allowed to over-trigger. Phase 0 prevents that.

rule.statement: If the user's most recent message does NOT contain an explicit brainstorm trigger word AND no prior message in this session approved a brainstorm, the orchestrator MUST stop before Phase 1 and call AskUserQuestion.
rule.rationale: Auto-routed brainstorm without consent burns the user's trust and quota on output they did not ask for. Explicit triggers: "brainstorm", "10 ideas", "audit", "diverge-converge", "what could we improve", "give me options", "список ідей", "аудит", "що покращити".

rule.statement: The AskUserQuestion payload MUST present exactly two options: "Run brainstorm" (10 research + 8 review agents, full diverge-converge-review) and "Short answer" (2-3 sentence direct recommendation, no agents).
rule.rationale: Two clear options collapse the decision; more options invite indecision. The cost figure (10+8 agents) is shown so the user understands the magnitude.

rule.statement: On "Short answer" the orchestrator MUST exit the workflow and answer inline. On "Run brainstorm" it proceeds to Phase 1 and records the approval for the rest of the session.
rule.rationale: Recording the approval prevents re-asking on every follow-up turn within the same brainstorm. Exiting cleanly on "Short answer" avoids half-state.

section.p: Skip Phase 0 only when one of these is true: the current message contains an explicit trigger word, OR the session already saw a "Run brainstorm" approval, OR the workflow is invoked programmatically by another workflow that has its own consent contract.

section.title: Phase 1: DIVERGE (research agents)

rule.statement: Launch exactly N=10 research subagents with distinct personas, capped at 3 in parallel.
rule.rationale: 10 is the empirical sweet spot — fewer than 8 underfits coverage, more than 12 hits diminishing returns. Parallel limit of 3 keeps quota usage predictable and respects the session quota gate.

rule.statement: Each research agent MUST receive a distinct persona drawn from one strategy (by domain, by Six Thinking Hats, or by lens) and MUST produce exactly 30 recommendations in `state -> problem -> solution -> impact` shape.
rule.rationale: 30 is a forcing function: it pushes agents past the obvious top 5 into the long tail where novel ideas live. Distinct personas force independent search; identical personas yield identical lists (groupthink). The four-field schema makes downstream deduplication mechanical.

rule.statement: Research agents MUST run independently — no cross-pollination, no shared scratchpad, no awareness of other agents' output.
rule.rationale: Cross-pollination during diverge causes anchoring on whichever agent finishes first. Independence is the entire point of multi-agent diverge.

section.p: Persona assignment strategies are documented in `03-personas.xml`. Anti-patterns (identical prompts, too many agents, missing persona differentiation) are documented in `05-anti-patterns.xml`.

rule.statement: The synthesis pass is a single subagent (or the orchestrator itself if context budget allows) that performs four steps: deduplicate by clustering similar recommendations, count consensus (how many of the 10 agents independently surfaced each cluster), rank by composite impact (cost / effort / risk / user-impact), and emit priority tiers (This Week / Month / Quarter / Backlog).
rule.rationale: Without consensus counting, the highest-quantity agent wins by volume rather than signal. Tiering by horizon forces the synthesis to address actionability, not just impact.

rule.statement: Consensus count MUST be reported as an integer in [1, 10] for every surviving recommendation.
rule.rationale: The reviewer phase uses consensus count as one input to re-tiering. It must be auditable.

section.title: Phase 3: REVIEW (adversarial reviewers)

rule.statement: Launch exactly M=8 reviewer subagents in parallel-capped waves of 3, each assigned exactly one attack vector from the required list.
rule.rationale: One vector per reviewer prevents the "polite generalist" failure mode where every reviewer waters down their critique. The eight required vectors cover the canonical failure modes: feasibility, security, architecture, solo-dev DX, cost-benefit, missing perspectives, domain depth, prioritization synthesis.

rule.statement: Reviewer prompts MUST be adversarial: the reviewer is told to find what is wrong, not to confirm what is right.
rule.rationale: "Review this" is rubber-stamping. "Attack this from vector X" surfaces actual blockers. The eight vectors are listed in `04-reviewer-roles.xml`.

section.p: Required reviewer roles and their one-line attack vectors are enumerated in `04-reviewer-roles.xml`. Optional roles (e.g. accessibility, regulatory, performance) may be added when the domain warrants.

rule.statement: Re-tier every recommendation per reviewer consensus: a recommendation that survived 8/10 research agents but was attacked by 5/8 reviewers MUST drop a tier or move to backlog with explicit "why defer" rationale.
rule.rationale: Without re-tiering, reviewer feedback is decorative. Re-tiering makes it load-bearing.

rule.statement: The final output MUST include: re-tiered priority list, adjusted effort estimates, devil's-advocate items the reviewers flagged but the user should still see, cost projection if relevant, and explicit "why defer" for every backlog item.
rule.rationale: The user needs to see what was rejected and why, not just what survived. "Why defer" is the artifact that prevents re-litigating the same idea next quarter.

rule.statement: Self-evaluation pass MUST be appended: Coverage (all perspectives covered?), Novelty (any surprising recs?), Actionability (executable now?), Bias (groupthink? infrastructure-bias?), User focus (≥20% user-facing?).
rule.rationale: Self-evaluation is a cheap groupthink detector. If novelty is low and bias is "infrastructure-only", the persona assignment failed and the next brainstorm should adjust.

list.item: parallel_limit: 3 (concurrency cap respected by both diverge and review).
